There is a relationship between 3D point cloud scene understanding and semantic segmentation, as the latter is a sub-field or technique within the former.
There is a relationship between 3D point cloud scene understanding and object detection, as both are areas of study within the broader field of scene understanding.
A 3D scene graph represents the environment as a graph, where nodes represent objects and edges characterize the relationships between object pairs.
A 3D scene graph abstracts the environment as a graph, indicating a relationship between the two concepts.
The relationships between objects are characterized by edges that link object pairs in a 3D scene graph, demonstrating a clear relationship between the two concepts.
3D scene structures are inherently hierarchical, implying a hierarchical relationship between the two concepts.
Instances can have multiple supports, such as lamps standing on a table, chairs supported by the floor, and the floor itself having no support.
A growing body of works suggest that complex relationship information is reasoned hierarchically and systemically according to the role of the prefrontal cortex.
Relationships such as support can be extracted rapidly and influence other relationships in the perceptual process.
A TV and a sofa are related since they serve the function of 'watching TV' together.
The table easily supports the TV and can be used as a bridge to predict the 'front' relationship with the sofa, both of which are supported by the floor.
The table supports the TV and is also used to predict the 'front' relationship with the sofa, where both table and sofa are supported by the floor.
The underlying hierarchical structures in 3D scenes are label free and reliable, playing an essential role in scene understanding at no additional cost.
Existing 3D scene graph prediction models are oblivious to the underlying structures in the point cloud scenes.
KISG [47] proposes a graph auto-encoder to learn a closed set and ground truth prior knowledge from relationship triplets in data for 3D scene graph prediction.

KISG [47] captures relevant prior knowledge from text-only ground truth labels, which merely contain facts expressed by label descriptions while lacking complex but indispensable multimodal knowledge for 3D scene graph prediction.
We show that the implicit hierarchical structure correlations between object pairs and their relationships can be explicitly represented by a knowledge base.
We propose a 3D spatial multimodal knowledge accumulation module to explicitly merge the hierarchical structures of 3D scenes into the network to strengthen the 3D scene graph prediction process.

A graph reasoning network is proposed to correlate 3D spatial visual contents of scenes with textual facts, bridging the gap between symbolic knowledge and visual information.
A graph reasoning network is incorporated into the relationships prediction stage as extra guidance, utilizing vision-relevant 3D spatial multimodal knowledge.
The distribution of possible relationships of object pairs is effectively regularized, making the predictions less ambiguous.
The regular patterns of 3D physical spaces are unified with the deep architecture to facilitate 3D scene graph prediction.
The hierarchical symbolic knowledge construction module exploits extra knowledge as the baseline to admit the hierarchical structure cues of 3D scenes.
The visual context encoding module constructs hierarchical visual graph and learns contextualized features by a region-aware graph network.
The 3D spatial multimodal knowledge accumulation module is proposed to regularize the semantic space of relationship prediction.
The semantic space is regularized to enhance the prediction of relationships.
The learned knowledge and proposed modules together enhance the prediction performance of 3D scene graphs.
Scene graph generation from 2D images has been a topic of interest in the vision community.
In mainstream approaches, objects are first detected and then classified based on their relationships.
The proposed method extends 2D scene graphs to 3D spaces, requiring alterations in representation, architecture, and training.

The external knowledge graph and the hierarchical symbolic knowledge graph are merged to bring the hierarchical structures in 3D spaces into the external knowledge graph.
Each node in the external knowledge graph is classified with a hierarchical token to distinguish the layers in the hierarchical structure.
The hierarchical token of each node denotes its corresponding layer in the hierarchical structure.
Each node is initialized as the concatenation of its trainable hierarchical token.
Each node is initialized as the concatenation of its trainable hierarchical token and the word2vec (GloVe [26]) representation of the object category.

Since the hierarchical structure of 3D spaces is built based on the physical support relationships between objects, we add additional edges representing support relationships between nodes to the external knowledge graph Ke.
Specifically, we define a new edge type: given two nodes si and sj, we connect si to sj using a support edge to represent the physical support relationship between si and sj.
By definition, each node in the hierarchical structure is supported by the node in neighboring layers. Therefore, we add a support edge between two correlated nodes in neighboring layers.
Each edge is initialized as the trainable GloVe representation of its edge type.
Finally, we formulate the updated external knowledge graph as hierarchical symbolic knowledge graph Ks.

Taking a scene point cloud with object instance annotations as input, we build a hierarchical visual graph Gv = {Vv,Ev} where Vv and Ev denotes object instances and edges of object pairs respectively, under the guidance of the hierarchical symbolic knowledge graph Ks.
Then, a region-aware graph network is employed to propa-
The guidance of the hierarchical symbolic knowledge graph Ks leads to the employment of a region-aware graph network to propagate node messages through the visual graph Gv.
The node messages are propagated through the visual graph Gv in order to learn the contextualized feature representation using a region-aware graph network.
Point Cloud Transformer [13] is used to extract spatial-aware visual features fv for each object instance.
An MLP is used to lift the parameters of each bounding box (i.e., center and size) to feature space for encoding the spatial features ft.
The semantic features fw for each object are assigned using an embedding table initialized by GloVe [26].
Each node in the visual graph is initialized as the concatenation of features fv, ft, and fw.

The edge set Ev of visual graph Gv is completed by extracting potential physical relationships between nodes in the adjacent layers based on the corresponding nodes in the hierarchical symbolic knowledge graph Ks.
Spatial interactions between node pairs are modeled and encoded using an MLP for the initial edge embedding of node pairs.
In the context, the spatial interactions between node pairs are modeled and the initial edge embedding for node pairs is encoded using an MLP.
Objects sharing the same physical support are correlated because they have a similar functional role in the environment and are typically in close proximity to each other.
Pillow and clothes are usually supported by a bed, emphasizing the relationship between these objects within the context.
The region-aware graph network is proposed to jointly highlight the interrelated regions of each node in the visual graph Gv and encode the hierarchical contexts of the input scene.
The contextual regions around each node in the visual graph Gv can be defined as other nodes sharing the same physical support, which helps to enrich the hidden state of each node.
In the region-aware graph network, messages are passed along the edges in the visual graph Gv to iteratively update the hidden state of each node vi, vj at each time step t.
The enriched hidden state ij for edge (vi, vj) is computed by the formula ˜he,tij = he,tij +ψ(ho,tk ) +ψ(ho,ts)
After feature representation enhancements, the message passing of nodes and edges can be formulated as ho,t+1i= GRU (˜ho,ti, mo,ti) and he,t+1ij= GRU (˜he,tij , me,tij)
The calculation of the message for each node is mo,ti =(φn(˜ho,tj ) + φe(˜he,tij )), where mo,tij are the incoming messages for updating each node and edge
For each edge, the hidden state of subject and object node is transformed by two MLPs before fusing them to obtain the message me,tij = φs(˜ho,ti ) + φo(˜ho,tj)
The final hidden states of nodes and edges are taken as the contextual feature coij for each edge (vi, vj) ∈ Ev and for each node vi ∈ Vv

An MLP is used as object detection head to predict confident initial class guesses given the contextual node features.

The three most confident object categories are selected for the subject and object nodes in the visual graph, based on initial guesses, before retrieving the multimodal knowledge embedding using the predicted categories.

The method proposed is for 3D scene graph prediction from raw point clouds.
The method explores regular patterns of 3D physical spaces into the deep network.
Hierarchical symbolic knowledge is first reconstructed via exploiting external knowledge.
A knowledge-guided visual context encoding module builds a hierarchical visual graph and learns the contextualized features by a region-aware graph network.
A 3D spatial multimodal knowledge accumulation module is proposed to regularize the semantic space of relationship prediction.
Extensive experiments on the 3DSSG dataset show that the method outperforms existing state-of-the-art.
The method can mitigate the effect of data imbalance and label noises.
In the future, the plan is to exploit the attributes of 3D objects to build richer knowledge graphs.
The aim is to improve the prediction performances of attribute-focused relationships.
